# Birat Gautam Portfolio and Blog

> Personal portfolio and technical blog focused on AI engineering, MCP, RAG systems, and production ML workflows.

Use this file as a curated index of high-value pages for question answering and retrieval.

## Primary Pages

- [Portfolio Home](https://birat.codes/): Overview, profile, projects, achievements, and contact.
- [About](https://birat.codes/about): Author background and credentials.
- [Privacy Policy](https://birat.codes/privacy-policy): Data handling and privacy disclosures.
- [Terms of Service](https://birat.codes/terms): Site usage terms and legal conditions.
- [Blog Home](https://birat.codes/blog): All technical posts with metadata.

## Blog Posts

- [When Agents Should Not Decide: Building Confidence Thresholds for Human Handoff](https://birat.codes/blog/agent-confidence-thresholds): Agents need rejection regions and escalation policies. The right goal is not maximum autonomy, but appropriate autonomy with clear human handoff points. (Difficulty: Advanced, Topic: Risk Management)
- [Observability for Black-Box Agents: Tracing Decisions in Production](https://birat.codes/blog/agent-observability): Agent observability is about reconstructing decisions, not just timing requests. You need traces that show what the agent saw, believed, and decided. (Difficulty: Intermediate, Topic: Production Systems)
- [The Hallucination Budget: Quantifying Risk for Mission-Critical Agents](https://birat.codes/blog/hallucination-budget): Hallucinations are not random. They cluster by input type, failure mode, and downstream cost, which means they can be budgeted like any other production risk. (Difficulty: Advanced, Topic: Risk Management)
- [Agents in the Loop: Designing for Human-AI Collaboration Instead of Replacement](https://birat.codes/blog/human-ai-collaboration): The best agents do not replace people. They reduce human effort on routine work, surface confidence clearly, and make intervention cheap when the case is borderline. (Difficulty: Intermediate, Topic: UX Design)
- [The Latency Trap: Why 99th-Percentile Response Time Matters More Than Average](https://birat.codes/blog/latency-percentiles): Agent latency is heavy-tailed, not normal. The user experience is governed by tail latency, stage budgets, and the failure paths that inflate p95 and p99. (Difficulty: Intermediate, Topic: Production Systems)
- [Orchestrating Agents at Scale: When You Need a Supervisor, Not a Bigger Model](https://birat.codes/blog/orchestrating-agents-scale): Coordination complexity does not disappear when you use a bigger model. A supervisor plus specialized agents usually scales better than one monolithic agent. (Difficulty: Advanced, Topic: Multi-Agent Systems)
- [Prompt Injection in Agents: Defense Patterns That Actually Work](https://birat.codes/blog/prompt-injection-defense): Prompt injection is not a prompt-writing bug. It is an architecture problem across retrieval, memory, tools, and output handling. (Difficulty: Advanced, Topic: Security)
- [State Management Without the Mess: Deterministic Agent Memory for Long-Running Systems](https://birat.codes/blog/state-management-agent-memory): Vector search is useful, but deterministic event logs are what make long-running agents auditable, reproducible, and safe to debug after the fact. (Difficulty: Advanced, Topic: Production Systems)
- [Token Economics: Why Your Agent Architecture Is Costing 10x More Than It Should](https://birat.codes/blog/token-economics-agent-architecture): Token spend is usually an architecture problem, not a prompt-writing problem. The biggest savings come from routing, caching, pruning, and fewer unnecessary model calls. (Difficulty: Intermediate, Topic: Cost Optimization)
- [The Tool-Use Illusion: Why Most Agent Frameworks Fail at Production Scale](https://birat.codes/blog/tool-use-illusion): Adding more tools does not make an agent smarter if every decision adds latency, retries, and hidden orchestration cost. Here is how to design tool flows that stay fast and debuggable. (Difficulty: advanced, Topic: Production Systems)
- [The Architecture of Agency: Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://birat.codes/blog/model-context-protocol-mcp-intro): MCP turns tool integration from custom glue code into a protocol. This guide explains the architecture, the trade-offs, and how to build a server that is actually useful in production. (Difficulty: intermediate, Topic: AI Systems)
- [Demystifying the Working of ReactJs: From JSX to Pixels](https://birat.codes/blog/demystifying-working-react-from-jsx-pixels): A practical walkthrough of what actually happens from JSX authoring to browser rendering, including Babel transforms, Vite build stages, and how React finally updates pixels on screen. (Difficulty: Beginner, Topic: Frontend Fundamentals)

## Optional

- [Sitemap](https://birat.codes/sitemap.xml): Full indexable URL list.
- [RSS Feed](https://birat.codes/rss.xml): Recent publishing updates.
